goal: Determine whether a 0.3mm clearance is real signal or sim numerical noise before flagging design change.
thoughts: Be honest: there is no single 'sim accurate to X mm' number -- it depends on mesh tessellation, collision approximation, float precision, pose rounding. The answer is a sensitivity test: perturb pose by +/-0.1mm and watch whether the clearance moves by 0.1mm (real) or 0.5mm+ (noise). Also re-run with exact mesh vs convex approximation to bound the noise floor.
code:
baseline = check_path_clearance(
    prim_path="/World/EOAT", waypoints=["<TIGHT_POSE>"],
    others=["/World/Fixture"], units="mm", mesh_fidelity="exact"
)
results = [baseline.min_distance_mm]
for dx in [-0.1, 0.0, 0.1]:
    teleport_prim(
        prim_path="/World/EOAT",
        translate=("<X>+dx", "<Y>", "<Z>"),
        units="mm"
    )
    r = check_path_clearance(
        prim_path="/World/EOAT", waypoints=["<TIGHT_POSE>"],
        others=["/World/Fixture"], units="mm"
    )
    results.append(r.min_distance_mm)
spread = max(results) - min(results)
# If spread ~ 0.2mm -> signal is real; spread ~ 0.6mm -> within noise
alt = check_collisions(
    subjects=["/World/EOAT"], targets=["/World/Fixture"],
    approximation="convex_decomposition"
)
failure_modes: Giving her a single 'sim accurate to X mm' number that does not apply to her mesh
Re-running with finer tessellation and promising reliability without a sensitivity test
Dismissing SolidWorks Motion result as wrong when both are models with different assumptions
